title: Mango body butter
subtitle: A whipped anhydrous body butter with mango butter, shea, and sweet almond oil
excerpt: Mango butter melts at skin temperature and absorbs quickly, leaving a non-greasy finish. Whipped with shea butter and sweet almond oil and scented with mango fragrance oil, this body butter suits dry skin on the body and heels. No water means no preservative needed.
sourceNotes: Mango butter properties and cosmetic use: INCI reference Mangifera indica (mango) seed butter. Anhydrous formulation stability: EU Cosmetics Regulation (EC) No 1223/2009, preservative requirement exemption for water-free products. Whipping technique for body butters: Susan Miller Cavitch, The Soapmaker's Companion (1997), balm and butter formulations.

recipe.batchNotes: Ratios hold at double batch. Whip in batches of no more than 400 g; larger quantities take much longer to whip to the right consistency.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Body butter keeps 12 months. Make a full 300 g batch and store in two jars.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 12 months in a cool dark place. Discard if the butter develops a sharp or paint-like smell (rancid oils) or visible discolouration.

glossaryTerms.definition: A preparation that contains no water phase. Anhydrous balms and butters do not need a preservative because there is no water for microbes to grow in.

content.text: Mango butter is a solid vegetable fat extracted from the kernels of mango fruit. It melts close to skin temperature, which gives it a quick-absorbing, light feel compared to heavier butters like cocoa or kokum. Combined with shea butter for moisture retention and sweet almond oil to lighten the texture, this
body butter keeps 12 months without a preservative. The method is: melt, chill until semi-solid, then whip to a light cream texture.

items.prepNote: 2% of batch; added when butter is cooled to 40 degrees C

content.text: Melt the mango butter and shea butter together over a water bath or in a heatproof bowl set over a saucepan of simmering water. Once melted, add the sweet almond oil and stir to combine. Remove from heat.

Pour the melted blend into a mixing bowl and place in the freezer for 20 to 30 minutes, or in the refrigerator for about 1 hour. The mixture should be firm around the edges and semi-solid in the centre. If the bowl is fully solid, let it soften at room temperature for 10 minutes before whipping.

Using a hand mixer or a stand mixer with a whisk attachment, beat the semi-solid butter on medium speed for 2 to 3 minutes until it lightens and becomes fluffy. Add the arrowroot powder and the fragrance oil if using, and whip for a further 1 minute. The finished texture should be light and airy, not oily or lumpy.

Spoon the whipped butter into a clean jar. The texture is best used within 48 hours of making; after that it will firm slightly as the butters re-solidify at room temperature. In a warm room it may soften but will not melt. In a cold kitchen in winter, let it soften before applying.
Apply a small amount to the inner forearm and wait 24 to 48 hours before applying to larger areas. This is standard practice when using any new cosmetic formulation.

Keep in a cool dark cupboard. If the butter melts in warm weather, chill and re-whip. The whipped texture won't return perfectly but the product remains usable. Discard if the butter smells sharp or paint-like. Keeps 12 months.
